links.targetTitle: DocumentToolbar has three independent layers of styling control
DocumentToolbar exposes className enabledControls and callback props

title: Revision History panel styling requires scoped CSS on document-editor-context since no props are exposed
content:
The revision history panel is rendered by Connect (not the editor) when the user clicks the history button. It lives inside `#document-editor-context` in the DOM, which means the editor component has no React-level control over its appearance.

To style it, use CSS in the project's `style.css` scoped to the document type:

```css
#document-editor-context[data-document-type="your/document-type"] {
  background-color: #1e1e2e;
  color: #cdd6f4;
}
```

Key CSS targets inside the revision history panel:

- `.bg-slate-50` / `[class*="bg-gray-50"]` — header pills (DOC ID, BRANCH, scope)
- `article` — revision cards
- `.rounded-md.bg-slate-50` — timeline content area
- `.border-slate-100` — timeline left border
- `h2.bg-slate-50` — day separator headers
- `.text-gray-900`, `.text-stone-300`, `.text-gray-600` — text colors
- `.border-gray-200`, `.bg-white` — borders and backgrounds

All overrides require `!important` since they compete with Connect's own Tailwind utility classes.

The `data-document-type` attribute selector is critical — it scopes your styles to only your editor type, preventing them from leaking into other editors in the same Connect instance.
description: The revision history panel is rendered by Connect outside the editor tree. It lives in #document-editor-context and can only be themed via scoped CSS using data-document-type attribute selectors.
